title: Synthetic Identity Activated Through Commercial Mail Receiving Agency Address
narrative: A synthetic identity is established using a commercial mail receiving agency (CMRA) address disguised as a residential location. The account ages 4-6 months with small authorized user tradeline piggybacking before activation. Initial transactions are conservative: $47-$89 purchases at grocery/gas MCCs with consistent addr1 and dist1 values suggesting local activity. After the aging period, a sudden bust-out occurs with 8-12 high-value transactions ($850-$2,400) within 72 hours across electronics and jewelry merchants. The R_emaildomain switches from free provider to a burner domain registered 30 days prior. Card velocity metrics spike: card1_txn_count_7day jumps from 2-3 to 15+, while card1_amt_zscore exceeds 4.5 standard deviations. The CMRA address often correlates with addr1 values appearing across 20+ unrelated accounts in the dataset.
indicators: addr1 matches known CMRA location appearing in 20+ distinct customer profiles
Account aging period of 4-6 months with transaction amounts under $100, then sudden spike to $850-$2,400
card1_txn_count_7day increases from baseline 2-3 to 15+ transactions in 72-hour window
card1_amt_zscore exceeds 4.5 during bust-out phase versus 0.8-1.2 during aging
R_emaildomain changes from established free provider to domain registered within 30 days
dist1 consistency during aging (95%+ transactions within 15km) then geographic scatter across 200+ km
MCC shift from everyday categories (5411, 5541) to high-risk electronics/jewelry (5732, 5944) within 48 hours
summary: An account uses a mail-forwarding address shared by 20+ other accounts, makes small local purchases for 4-6 months, then suddenly executes 15+ transactions totaling thousands of dollars in 72 hours while switching to a newly-registered email domain.
reasoning: Fraudsters build a fake credit history using a commercial mailbox to receive cards, age the account to appear legitimate, then drain maximum credit before the bust-out is detected—the address reuse and coordinated timing across multiple accounts reveal an organized synthetic identity operation.
indicator_explanations: Address is a mail drop shared by dozens of supposedly unrelated accounts
Account stayed quiet with small purchases, then suddenly maxed out in one burst
Transaction frequency exploded from a few per week to over fifteen in three days
Purchase amounts jumped to extreme outliers compared to the card's historical pattern
Email domain switched from Gmail or Yahoo to a brand-new suspicious domain
Card stayed local for months then suddenly scattered across distant cities
Purchases shifted from groceries and gas to expensive electronics and jewelry overnight
